frontmatter.title: Ray Theory I: Snell’s Law and the Ray Parameter

1. Plane waves and interfaces: the physical picture

children.label: 1. Plane waves and interfaces: the physical picture

children.value:
Consider a plane seismic wave propagating through a homogeneous medium.
Wavefronts are surfaces of constant phase, and
When such a wave encounters a
These constraints force rays to bend.

children.alt: Schematic of a plane wave incident on a horizontal interface showing ray angle and wavefront spacing

children.value: Plane wave incident on a horizontal interface. The ray angle θ is measured from vertical, and wavefront spacing Δs relates to horizontal spacing Δx through the ray parameter.

children.value: This is why ray theory works so naturally for layered Earth models.

children.alt: Wavefronts crossing an interface between two layers with different velocities

children.value: Snell’s law as timing continuity. Wavefronts (colored by time progression from early to late) bend across the interface, preserving arrival time continuity. The ray parameter p = u sin θ remains constant across the boundary.

children.value: This single idea explains why travel-time curves encode depth information.

children.alt: Curved ray paths in a velocity model increasing with depth

children.value: Ray paths in a model with velocity increasing with depth. Rays with different takeoff angles (different ray parameters p) turn at different depths. Steeper rays (smaller p) penetrate deeper before returning to the surface.

children.alt: Travel-time curve with tangent line showing ray parameter

children.value: Travel-time curve T(X) and ray parameter. The slope of the tangent line at any point equals the ray parameter: p = dT/dX. This geometric relationship connects observable travel times to ray paths.
